clinical_history: 52-year-old man with one acute and one "chronic" finding.  Leads V1-V6 are actually leads V1R through V6R (right chest leads).  What's going on?
diagnosis: Acute inferior/posterior/lateral myocardial infarction (MI) in a man with situs inversus-type dextrocardia.  The limb leads look like they have been reversed, but they actually have not.  The right-sided chest leads show normal R wave progression because of the dextrocardia.  Note ST elevations  II, III, aVF (lead III is actually lead II, and vice versa), and right-sided leads V5 and V6. ST depressions in right-sided V1-V3 are likely reciprocal to postero-lateral ST elevations.  Finally, to obtain a "corrected"  ECG, leads aVL and aVR should be reversed, and lead I polarity also reversed (so it  then reveals a positive P, R wave, and ST depressions), and II and II reversed as noted. The PR is slightly prolonged at about 210 ms.
